title: SK Hynix debuts on Nasdaq, raising $26.5B in second-largest US listing ever by a foreign company
subtitle: The world's leading maker of high-bandwidth memory closed up nearly 13% on its first day, giving US investors direct exposure to the chip layer that sits next to every Nvidia AI accelerator.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: SK Hynix listed American depositary receipts on Nasdaq under the ticker SKHY on July 10, raising about $26.5 billion in an offering priced the prior week. Shares closed up roughly 13% on the first day of trading, according to Fortune and CNBC. It is the second-largest US listing ever by a foreign company and the largest US share sale of 2026 outside of SpaceX's June IPO.

The listing matters because SK Hynix controls roughly 60% of the high-bandwidth memory market — the vertically stacked DRAM that sits next to Nvidia's AI accelerators and gates how fast a training run or inference query can actually move data. Every hyperscaler capex dollar going into GPU clusters is partly a bet on SK Hynix's HBM roadmap, and until this listing, US public-market investors could only reach that exposure through Nvidia itself or through Korean ADRs with limited float.

The debut caps a run in which SK Hynix's Korean shares climbed several hundred percent in 2026 alone, its market cap crossed $1 trillion in late May, and its Q1 2026 revenue approached ₩52.6 trillion with operating margin above 70%. The offering also gives the company dollar-denominated capital ahead of the 2027–2028 HBM4 and HBM4E cycles, where Micron and Samsung are both trying to close the gap.

Takeaway for learners: when studying the AI stack, do not stop at the model layer. The bottleneck for training frontier models today is not compute alone — it is memory bandwidth, and one Korean company sells most of it. Following the memory-chip supply chain teaches you more about where AI capacity actually comes from than another read of a benchmark table.
